extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of speech outside public meals
verbatim: To each one of those who comes in to the public meals the eldest man says, as he points to the doors, Through these no word goes out.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific institutional rule at Spartan syssitia (public meals) where conversation was strictly confidential, enforced by the older men against all participants (predominantly the younger citizens being socialized).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to use torches at night
verbatim: The Spartans, after drinking in moderation at their public meals, go away without a torch. In fact, they are not permitted to walk with a light either on this route or on any other, so that they may become accustomed to travelling in darkness at night confidently and fearlessly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is a general prohibition applied to the Spartan citizen group to foster military readiness and nocturnal navigation skills.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to non-practical books and treatises
verbatim: They learned to read and write for purely practical reasons; but all other forms of education they banned from the country, books and treatises being included in this quite as much as men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Spartan state explicitly restricted the intellectual resources available to its citizens, limiting education to literacy for utility and banning theoretical or literary works.

extracted_rules.rule: Civic rights loss for pederastic misconduct
verbatim: Affectionate regard for boys of good character was permissible, but embracing them was held to be disgraceful, on the ground that the affection was for the body and not for the mind. Any man against whom complaint was made of any disgraceful embracing was deprived of all civic rights for life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This describes a legal consequence (loss of atimia/civic status) for a specific social transgression, defining the boundary of acceptable citizen behavior.

verbatim: Moreover, the young men were required not only to respect their own fathers and to be obedient to them, but to have regard for all the older men, to make room for them on the streets, to give up their seats to them, and to keep quiet in their presence. As the result of this custom each man had authority, not as in other states over his own children, slaves, and property, but also over his neighbour’s in like manner as over his own.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that age granted a Spartan man the resource of authority over any youth, not just his own family, effectively reducing the independence of all young men.

verbatim: Whosoever of the citizens would not submit to the discipline to which the boys were subjected had no participation in civic rights.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Participation in the Spartan agoge (discipline) was a prerequisite for maintaining the status and resources associated with citizenship.

verbatim: It was not allowed them to go abroad, so that they should have nothing to do with foreign ways and undisciplined modes of living.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Spartan citizens were explicitly restricted from leaving their territory to prevent cultural contamination.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to reside in the country
verbatim: Lycurgus also introduced the practice of banning all foreigners from the country, so that these should not filter in and serve to teach the citizens something bad.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This describes the known Spartan policy of xenelasia, where non-citizens were barred from the state to preserve social ideological purity.

extracted_rules.resource: Exclusive use of owned property and slaves
verbatim: but it was their custom to use the neighbours’ servants as their own if they needed them and also their dogs and horses, unless the owners required them for their own use. And in the country, if anyone found himself lacking anything and had need of it, he would open an owner’s storehouse and take away enough to meet his need.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Spartan society, individual property rights were weakened in favor of communal access among the citizen class (the Peers).

verbatim: They used to make the Helots drunk and exhibit them to the young as a deterrent from excessive drinking.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a systemic practice of using the enslaved group (Helots) as negative moral examples through forced degradation.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to temples and religious services
verbatim: [§36] The temples and religious services were open to maidens and youths alike.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a general religious rule in Spartan society where unmarried females (maidens) and young males (youths) were granted equal access to sacred sites and ceremonies.

verbatim: [§41] The boys in Sparta were lashed with whips during the entire day at the altar of Artemis Orthia, frequently to the point of death, and they bravely endured this, cheerful and proud, vying with one another for the supremacy as to which one of them could endure being beaten for the longer time and the greater number of blows.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plutarch describes a specific, recurring institutional practice (The Flagellation) where Spartan boys are subjected to extreme physical violence as a test of endurance, indicating they lack the resource of protection from corporal punishment.

verbatim: [§42] One of the noble and blessed privileges which Lycurgus appears to have secured for his fellow-citizens was abundance of leisure. In fact it was not permitted them to take up any menial trade at all; and there was no need whatever of making money, which involves a toilsome accumulation, nor of busy activity, because of his having made wealth wholly unenvied and unhonoured.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Spartan law (attributed to Lycurgus) explicitly prohibited citizens from engaging in trade or manual labor, reserving their time for leisure and civic duties.

verbatim: [§42] The Helots tilled the soil for them, paying a return which was regularly settled in advance. There was a ban against letting for a higher price, so that the Helots might make some profit, and thus be glad to do the work for their masters, and so that the masters might not look for any larger return.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines a legal restriction on masters (a ban on raising rent) which allowed the Helots, a servile group, to keep any agricultural surplus beyond the fixed rate, providing them with a limited form of economic protection.

verbatim: [§43] It was forbidden them to be sailors and to fight on the sea. Later, however, they did engage in such battles, and, after they had made themselves masters of the sea, they again desisted, since they observed that the character of the citizens was deteriorating sadly.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes a traditional Spartan rule that prohibited its citizens from engaging in naval activities or serving as sailors, based on the belief it negatively impacted their character.
